input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** is a **BIM-based Digital Twin (DT) framework** designed for **Asset Engineering and Construction (AECO)** domains, specifically targeting **BIM-based infrastructure projects**. The physical twin is modeled as a **scalable, service-oriented operational control system** that bridges **design-time BIM models** with **real-time runtime operations** of physical assets (e.g., buildings, structures, or construction components).

  **Key Components & Operational Domain:**
  - **Core Architecture:** Operates within the **BIM lifecycle**, leveraging semantically rich, object-based 3D models (e.g., IFC, COBie) to represent physical assets with geometric, spatial, functional, and performance attributes.
  - **Operational Domain:** Focuses on **construction, facility management, and infrastructure lifecycle phases**, where BIM serves as the foundational data framework. The DT integrates **real-time sensor data, simulation outputs, and operational logs** to enable predictive and prescriptive analytics.
  - **Key Characteristics for Digital Twinning:**
    - **Semantic Integration:** Relies on **BIM’s object-oriented structure** to ensure interoperability between design models (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) and runtime DT components, enabling **clash detection, performance simulation, and collaborative workflows**.
    - **Real-Time Connectivity:** Enables **live synchronization** between physical assets and digital representations via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto protocols**, facilitating **low-latency data exchange** (e.g., IoT sensor streams, actuator commands).
    - **Scalability & Extensibility:** Adopts a **modular architecture** with **defined extension points** (e.g., service-oriented interfaces) to support **domain-specific DT extensions** (e.g., energy optimization, structural monitoring) without disrupting core functionality.
    - **Model-Driven Automation:** Uses **Design Science Research (DSR)** methodologies to develop **reproducible DT deployments**, reducing manual intervention via **automated BIM-to-DT mapping pipelines** (e.g., rule-based transformation workflows).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin provides **core operational services** aligned with AECO domain requirements, leveraging **real-time data synchronization** and **service-oriented architecture**:

  - **Monitoring:** Real-time **asset performance tracking** via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** for live data streams (e.g., sensor inputs like temperature, occupancy, or structural strain).
  - **Optimization:** **Runtime control adjustments** (e.g., HVAC system tuning, energy efficiency optimization) based on BIM-derived constraints and operational feedback.
  - **Prediction:** **Performance forecasting** for critical assets (e.g., structural integrity, energy consumption) using **BIM-derived models** and real-time data.
  - **Control:** **Automated operational commands** (e.g., dynamic adjustments to building systems) via **service-oriented APIs** (e.g., REST/gRPC) integrated with physical assets.
  - **Visualization:** **Interactive 3D/4D BIM integration** (e.g., Unreal Engine for immersive DT visualization) enabling architects/engineers to **collaboratively inspect**, simulate, and validate designs in real-time.

  **Target Users:**
  - **Design Teams:** For **clash detection**, **performance simulation**, and **collaborative design validation**.
  - **Operational Teams:** For **predictive maintenance**, **energy management**, and **runtime control optimization**.
  - **Project Managers:** For **workflow automation** and **scalable DT deployment** (e.g., automated deployment via model-driven frameworks).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on a **technically constrained yet purpose-built stack** for AECO DTs:

  - **Core Technologies:**
    - **Eclipse Hono/Ditto:** Enables **real-time data synchronization** between physical assets and digital twins via **IoT middleware** (e.g., live streaming of sensor data).
    - **Modular Architecture Framework:** Defined **extension points** for scalable service integration (e.g., service-oriented DT development in AECO).
    - **Automated Deployment:** Model-driven automation (e.g., **Design Science Research (DSR) artifacts**) to deploy DTs **reproducibly** with minimal manual intervention (e.g., C2 compliance).

  - **Design Science Methodology:**
    - **Requirements Elicitation:** Aligns DT features with **semantic integration**, **real-time connectivity**, and **extensibility** (e.g., BIM-based DTs for AECO projects).
    - **Prototyping/Experimentation:** Validates DT capabilities via **BIM-based infrastructure projects** (e.g., testing in AECO domains like building performance simulation).

  - **Not in Document:** No explicit mention of additional tools like Autodesk BIM 360, Revit, IFC, EnergyPlus, Dynamo, Navisworks, Python, DotNet, Neo4j, InfluxDB, AWS, Azure, or Unreal Engine beyond the context of **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** and **modular architecture**.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The Digital Twin (DT) framework enables **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **continuous synchronization** with minimal latency. While the exact synchronization frequency is not specified, the emphasis on **runtime configuration management** suggests adaptive temporal handling to accommodate dynamic operational needs. The modular architecture supports services with varying time scales, implying that different DT services may operate at different granularities (e.g., real-time monitoring vs. analytics-based predictions). Latency requirements are implied to be low due to the emphasis on **interactive visualization** and **bidirectional data exchange**, though precise thresholds are not detailed.

    Key temporal aspects:
    - Real-time synchronization (implied).
    - Adaptive runtime configuration for dynamic time scales.
    - Low-latency requirements for monitoring/control interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system supports a **modular, hierarchical architecture** where multiple **digital twin instances** can be deployed independently, each serving distinct operational scopes (e.g., per-floor, per-equipment, or enterprise-wide). Responsibilities are **centralized in a DT Framework hub**, enabling **bidirectional data exchange** between physical and digital components while allowing **decentralized DT Engineers** to extend capabilities via service integration. Coordination between instances relies on **standardized extension points** and **runtime configuration management**, ensuring low-overhead updates without disrupting the overall system. The system explicitly bridges the gap between BIM’s static models and dynamic operational needs by enabling **multi-level twin instantiation** (e.g., device-level, facility-level, or enterprise-level) with clear scopes of responsibility defined by roles like **Building Control Engineers** and **Facility Managers**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts a **coordinated, role-based architecture** where the **DT Framework** acts as a central orchestrator, facilitating **bidirectional data exchange** between enriched BIM models and operational DTs. Key roles include **Building Control Engineers** (for device modeling), **Facility Managers** (for real-time monitoring), and **DT Engineers** (for service integration). The system employs **modular architecture with standardized extension points** to support dynamic evolution, enabling seamless integration of custom analytics and services. Resource management is handled through **runtime configuration management**, allowing adaptive adjustments without redeployment. The orchestration ensures a **holistic life-cycle management** approach for buildings, integrating BIM’s static data with dynamic operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system ensures **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** via **standardized extension points** and **NF_X requirements** for scalability and maintainability. Integration with external systems (e.g., BIM tools, IoT devices, enterprise platforms) relies on **bidirectional data exchange protocols** enabled by the DT Framework’s modular design. Dependencies on external systems are managed through **low-overhead updates** and **runtime adaptability**, ensuring compatibility with varying technical expertise levels. The system supports **interoperability with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems** by leveraging the same orchestration patterns and data exchange mechanisms, facilitating a cohesive AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **model-driven engineering (MDE)** principles to integrate and manage computational models and data components throughout its lifecycle. The framework supports **three primary model types**:
1. **Geometric Models**: Enriched from Building Information Modeling (BIM) to represent physical building structures, including sensors, actuators, and control components, ensuring seamless integration with BIM’s static representations while addressing its limitations in dynamic BCS (Building Control Systems) modeling.
2. **Behavioral Models**: Defined by Building Control Engineers to implement control logic for sensors, actuators, and controllers, enabling direct implementation within BIM environments.
3. **Analytical Models**: Extracted via MDE to generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention, ensuring structured transformations from enriched BIM models.

**Data Components**:
- **Data Sources**: Primarily derived from BIM models and real-time synchronized data streams between physical infrastructure and digital twins.
- **Data Management**: Employs automated extraction and transformation pipelines to maintain a unified semantic foundation, supporting continuous synchronization between physical and digital systems.
- **Model Relationships**: Established through MDE principles to ensure interoperability between geometric, behavioral, and analytical models, facilitating modular extension via standardized extension points for custom services.

The DT constellation relies on these models and data to enable real-time data exchange, interactive visualization, and operational monitoring, with a focus on evolving with changing requirements through modular architecture.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Building Control Engineers define **physical actuators** and **control mechanisms** within the BIM environment, enabling direct implementation of control logic for building systems. Actuators (e.g., dampers, lighting systems, HVAC valves) are modeled alongside their control interfaces, allowing remote operation via the DT. The framework supports **standardized extension points** for integrating custom services, ensuring modularity and adaptability to evolving operational requirements. Safety constraints are implicitly enforced through the BIM-based control logic, though specific actuation ranges, limits, and failure modes are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The framework enables **sensing infrastructure** by allowing Building Control Engineers to define sensors (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, motion) directly within BIM models. These sensors are synchronized in real-time with physical assets via **Eclipse Hono/Ditto protocols**, ensuring continuous data flow. Spatial distribution is implicitly modeled through BIM geometry, though exact sensor placement granularity is unspecified. Sampling frequencies and transmission rates are not quantified, but the emphasis is on **real-time synchronization** to maintain DT fidelity. Measurement capabilities are inferred from control logic integration, with accuracy/precision tied to BIM’s static limitations and DT validation processes.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The framework addresses fidelity and validity through a **multi-layered approach** rooted in model-driven deployment principles:
1. **Fidelity Requirements**:
   - Ensures real-time synchronization between physical and digital twins via continuous data exchange, mitigating discrepancies caused by BIM’s static limitations.
   - Supports **automated model extraction** from enriched BIM data, reducing manual errors in semantic representation.

2. **Validation and Verification Methods**:
   - Leverages **MDE principles** to validate model transformations and artifact deployability with minimal manual intervention, ensuring structural integrity.
   - Implements **runtime monitoring** for system states, enabling immediate detection of deviations between physical and digital twins.

3. **Uncertainty Quantification**:
   - Addresses BIM’s inherent limitations in dynamic BCS components by integrating behavioral models defined by Building Control Engineers, reducing ambiguity in control logic.
   - Uses standardized extension points for custom services to dynamically adapt to operational uncertainties.

4. **Error Detection and Correction**:
   - Automated pipelines for data transformation and model extraction minimize errors, while modular architecture allows incremental updates to correct discrepancies.
   - **Quality assurance processes** are embedded in the framework’s lifecycle, ensuring compliance with operational requirements and stakeholder needs (e.g., Facility Managers, DT Engineers).

The validation framework emphasizes **theoretical and practical alignment** with research projects like TwinLight and BOREALIS, ensuring robustness across the DT lifecycle from modeling to runtime operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework enables **real-time data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital twins via **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** protocols, ensuring bidirectional synchronization. Data flows originate from enriched BIM models, where **asset-specific sensor data** (e.g., occupancy, energy consumption, environmental parameters) is extracted and preprocessed for consistency. Transmission occurs through **low-latency, event-driven mechanisms**, triggered by physical events (e.g., sensor activations, operational changes). Data quality is assured through **runtime validation** and **standardized extension points** for modular integration. The system supports **continuous synchronization** without requiring full redeployment, leveraging **runtime configuration management** for adaptability to evolving operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The framework facilitates bidirectional control flows to **Building Control Systems (BCS)** via bidirectional communication, enabling real-time operation of physical infrastructure through the DT. While the documents mention mechanisms for feeding control signals back to BCS, they do not specify authentication/validation mechanisms, feedback loops, conflict resolution strategies, or command execution confirmations. The described interaction is inherently bidirectional but lacks granular technical specifications for these critical aspects.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical network infrastructure employs **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for real-time data exchange, ensuring **low-latency, scalable communication** across heterogeneous systems. The architecture is **modular and service-oriented**, with standardized extension points for integrating custom services. The system prioritizes **interoperability** and **scalability**, supporting components from individual sensors to entire building infrastructure. While exact network topology or bandwidth specifications are not detailed, the framework’s **centralized hub-and-spoke model** (implied by the DT Framework’s role) enables efficient data routing. Security is implicitly addressed through the framework’s **runtime adaptability** and **low-overhead updates**, though explicit protocols (e.g., TLS, OAuth) are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The deployment model is **model-driven**, enabling scalable DT deployment via runtime configuration management. The framework supports **low-overhead updates** without full redeployment but does not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, or explicit scalability mechanisms. Availability/reliability assurances are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the AECO domain spans from **initial design** through **construction**, **operation**, and **decommissioning**, though the provided documents do not explicitly detail the decommissioning phase. Key phases include:
    - **Design Phase**: Models are created using **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** to represent physical assets with digital counterparts, ensuring consistency and scalability.
    - **Construction Phase**: DTs enable real-time synchronization between physical assets and digital models via **sensor networks**, facilitating modular construction and infrastructure monitoring.
    - **Operation Phase**: DTs support **life cycle management**, cost modeling, and operational efficiency through integration with AI and analytics, though fragmentation remains a challenge due to **fragmented, case-specific deployments**.
    Transition management relies on **bidirectional communication protocols**, but standardized schemas and governance frameworks are lacking, contributing to **high implementation costs** and **organizational barriers** (e.g., stakeholder misalignment).
    The lifecycle governance approach emphasizes **unified engineering workflows** and **interoperability standards** to address fragmentation, though no specific governance models (e.g., ISO 19650) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process leverages **open-source DT frameworks** like **Eclipse Ditto** and **BaSyx**, which provide infrastructure-level components but require **manual configuration** for analytics orchestration. AECO-specific frameworks, such as **Bentley’s iTwin.js**, are adapted via proprietary ecosystems or manual effort due to **low usability for non-developers**.
    Development methodologies focus on **modeling and workflow automation** to achieve consistent and scalable DT implementations, particularly for **modular construction and infrastructure monitoring**.
    Quality assurance practices are implied but not detailed; testing and validation rely on **integration with sensor networks and AI-driven analytics**, though no specific methodologies (e.g., OPC UA, AI-driven anomaly detection) are referenced.
    Version control and evolution management are not explicitly addressed, but the documents highlight the need for **robust integration frameworks** and **bidirectional communication protocols** to support DT evolution.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT generates **real-time insights** through integration with **sensor networks, AI, and predictive analytics**, enabling adaptive systems for operational efficiency. The documents reference **life cycle management** and **cost modeling** as key decision-support applications, though no specific analytics tools (e.g., AWS SageMaker) or data types (e.g., vibration, temperature) are mentioned.
    User interaction methods are implied to include **analytics dashboards** and **reinforcement learning-based decision-making** (e.g., for lighting systems in the BOREALIS project context), though the latter is not explicitly tied to DTs in the provided documents.
    Confidence measures for recommendations are not detailed, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** and **adaptive systems** suggests probabilistic or rule-based validation approaches are employed.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
Standardization efforts in AECO DTs focus on **fragmented data schemas** and **lack of unified engineering workflows**, hindering interoperability. The documents mention **AECO-specific standards like BIM** but do not specify versions (e.g., IFC 4.2/4.3) or compliance frameworks (e.g., COBie).
    Proprietary ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) and **high implementation costs** are cited as barriers, with no mention of certification requirements or interoperability standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT). The core challenge is the absence of **standardized data schemas** and **robust integration frameworks** to enable bidirectional communication across DT implementations.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: In the context of **Digital Twin (DT) systems within the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO) domain**, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **data governance policies, ownership frameworks, privacy protection mechanisms, compliance with data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA), consent management workflows, data anonymization techniques, or formalized sharing agreements**. While studies like Gerber et al.’s Living Lab DT emphasize **automated data acquisition and normalization** for BIM-based monitoring, these focus on **technical integration (e.g., real-time IoT/BIM synchronization)** rather than governance structures. The absence of **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) abstractions for data stewardship** (e.g., domain-specific metamodels for compliance tracking) further underscores a critical gap in **abstraction and reuse capabilities** for AECO DTs, as noted by Lehner et al.’s survey. Thus, **data ownership and privacy in AECO DTs remain unaddressed in the provided literature**, requiring custom or proprietary solutions (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js ecosystems) to handle these concerns manually. No specific tools, protocols, or regulatory frameworks are cited for privacy-preserving DT architectures in AECO.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents **do not explicitly address security and safety considerations** for AECO Digital Twin systems. While studies like Gerber et al.’s work highlight **data integration challenges** (e.g., stakeholder coordination) and **semantic drift** (Abbasi et al.), these focus on **model coherence and synchronization** rather than cybersecurity protocols. The absence of references to **authentication systems, access control mechanisms, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or risk assessment approaches** in DTs is notable. Recent proposals (e.g., Kamburjan et al.’s declarative life-cycle language) emphasize **dynamic evolution and rule-based management** of DT instances but do not detail security frameworks. Thus, **security and safety in AECO DTs are not explicitly modeled or governed in the surveyed literature**, leaving these aspects reliant on commercial tools (e.g., proprietary ecosystems) or ad-hoc adaptations.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately synthesizes the model-driven framework presented in the paper. It correctly identifies the domain (AECO), the core technology (BIM), specific integrations (Eclipse Hono, Ditto), and key architectural characteristics (modular, service-oriented, scalable) described in the abstract and introduction. It captures the essence of the research goal to bridge design-time BIM with runtime operations.

The description correctly identifies Monitoring, Control, and Visualization as core services, supported by the text's mention of 'real-time monitoring', 'interactive visualization' (via Unreal Engine), and the framework's support for 'bidirectional communication'. It correctly maps target users like 'Operational Teams' to 'Facility Managers' mentioned in the source. However, it goes beyond the source by specifying types of sensor data (e.g., 'occupancy, structural strain') and specific optimization tasks (e.g., 'HVAC system tuning') which are not detailed in the provided text. The text mentions 'simulation and life cycle-encompassing optimization' generally, but the specific examples are extrapolated.

This extraction is excellent and highly specific to the provided text. It correctly identifies 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for real-time data exchange and 'Unreal Engine' for visualization. It accurately describes the 'Modular Architecture Framework' with 'standardized extension points' and the use of 'Design Science Research (DSR)' methodology. The 'Not in Document' section correctly states that tools like Autodesk BIM 360, Revit, IFC, EnergyPlus, etc., are not mentioned in the text. The reasoning is strongly grounded in the source quotes.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
